metadata.title: Glossary
metadata.description: Alphabetical listing of key terms used throughout the Roof Systems Field Guide

terms.definition: Water that drains within 48 hours of rain stopping. The industry standard threshold for ponding that is not considered problematic.

Liquid-applied roof coating using acrylic chemistry. Lower cost than silicone, less ponding tolerance, more recoat flexibility with other chemistries. USA Flat Roof recognizes but does not typically lead with acrylic.

Observable indicators that a roof is moving through its service life: granule loss on shingles, chalking on coatings, plasticizer migration on PVC, blistering on modified bitumen, and similar system-specific patterns.
Older reflective coating applied over BUR or modified bitumen. 2–5 year life-extension product, not a true restoration system. Recognized but not typically proposed by USA Flat Roof.
Metal flashing at the downslope side of a chimney or curb, redirecting water away from the penetration.
Recognition heuristic using the home's architectural style and construction era to anticipate likely roof system before inspection.
Composition shingle with two layers bonded together creating a dimensional, shadowed appearance. Industry standard on new residential installs since the 2000s.
Fibrous mineral commonly used in pre-1980s roofing materials. Requires testing before disturbance and abatement procedures during tear-off on older buildings.
Plastic-bodied asphalt-based sealant used for repairs on BUR and modified bitumen.
Visible asphalt bead at seams where torch-down or hot-mopped modified bitumen has been installed. Distinguishes torch-down from cleaner self-adhered seams.
System of intake vents (typically at soffits) and exhaust vents (typically at ridge or upper roof) that allows air to flow through an attic, preventing heat and moisture buildup.
A company-defined level of decision-making authority granted to a sales rep, indicating what they may and may not commit to without escalation.
Closed-cell foam rod placed in joints before sealant application to control depth and improve sealant performance.
Attic ventilation configuration with appropriate proportions of intake and exhaust, allowing air to move through the full attic space.
First ply in a multi-ply roof system, attached to the substrate as the foundation for subsequent plies.
Closure detail at the eave on tile roofs preventing birds and pests from entering under tiles.
Bubble or bubble-cluster formation in roof membranes, typically caused by trapped moisture or air. Common on modified bitumen and BUR.
Flexible flashing component that wraps around a roof penetration (vent stack, plumbing pipe) to seal against the membrane or shingle.
Loss of flexibility in roofing materials due to age, UV exposure, or chemistry change. Indicates advanced aging.
Recognition heuristic using the building's purpose (restaurant, cold storage, etc.) to anticipate likely roof system.
Multi-ply asphalt-and-felt roofing system, often gravel-surfaced. Long field track record. USA Flat Roof restores, recovers, or replaces but does not install new BUR.
Top ply of a multi-ply roof system, often granule-surfaced for UV protection.
White powder appearance on coating surfaces, indicating UV-driven surface degradation. Approaching recoat window.
Water that consistently persists beyond 48 hours after rain. Disqualifies most restoration coatings.
Roofing tile made of fired clay (terracotta). Distinctive aesthetic, very long tile life, separate underlayment. Outside USA Flat Roof's standard scope.
Liquid-applied roof material that cures into a continuous waterproof layer on the roof surface. Includes silicone, acrylic, and aluminum-pigmented chemistries.
The base resin or material composition of a coating product, e.g., silicone, acrylic, polyurethane. Compatibility between coating chemistries during recoat is not guaranteed.
Asphalt-based shingle with mineral granule surface. Most common residential roof system in the United States.
In ventilation, the loss of insulation R-value when compressed; in flat-roof systems, the loss of slope when tapered insulation is crushed by foot traffic or equipment.
Heat moving through solid materials. One of the modes ventilation manages.
Top covering of a parapet wall, typically metal, designed to shed water and protect the wall assembly.
Small cross-section cut into a roof assembly to confirm layer count, substrate, or moisture content. Production team task.
Metal flashing installed over a base flashing (such as step flashing) to direct water away and protect the joint.
Sloped diversion built behind a chimney or curb to direct water around the obstruction.
Examining edges, terminations, or cut areas to assess multi-ply construction or substrate.
Raised platform on which rooftop equipment is mounted, requiring its own flashing details.
Light visible through small openings, indicating gaps in the roof or flashing assembly. Common attic-side indicator of penetration failures.
The structural surface beneath the roofing system. Wood sheathing on residential; concrete, metal, or wood on commercial.
In Good/Better/Best presentations, a tier included only to make another tier look more reasonable. Manipulative practice.
Premium composition shingle, typically multi-layer, designed to mimic slate, shake, or other specialty materials.
Reinforced coating layer at penetrations, transitions, and other high-stress areas, typically applied with embedded fabric.
Targeted repair work at penetrations, transitions, or specific failure locations rather than field-wide repair.
Specific conditions that make a roof unsuitable for restoration: wet insulation, chronic ponding, widespread substrate failure, asbestos, end-of-life condition, etc.
Metal ring that clamps a flat-roof membrane to a roof drain.
Metal flashing at the eave designed to direct water into the gutter rather than back behind the gutter into the fascia.
Aerial photography of a roof using a drone, valuable for multi-story commercial, tile roofs, and complex geometries. Supplements but does not replace hands-on inspection.
Flat-roof drainage off an unparapeted edge into a gutter.
White mineral deposits on masonry surfaces, indicating water has been moving through the masonry. Common around chimney bases.
Each face of the building (north, south, east, west). Documented during inspection.
Stage at which a roof system has exhausted its useful service life and replacement is the appropriate scope.
Single-ply rubber roof membrane, almost always black. Common on older commercial buildings nationally; smaller share of NorCal commercial. Limited USA Flat Roof scope.
Process of routing a finding, condition, or customer scenario beyond the rep's authorization to a senior estimator, production manager, or qualified inspector.
Outflow point of an attic ventilation system, typically at the ridge or upper roof.
Embedded fabric (typically polyester) used in coating systems at high-stress areas to bridge cracks, reinforce seams, and improve performance.
Insulation board used as a recover layer over BUR before TPO overlay, providing a smooth substrate.
Vertical board at the eave of a residential roof, behind which the gutter typically attaches.
The range of years a roof system typically performs in actual NorCal conditions, accounting for variation in installation, ventilation, exposure, and maintenance.
Localized seam separation forming a small bubble or open pocket. Common single-ply seam failure.
Metal or membrane component that directs water away from joints, transitions, and penetrations.
See Spray Polyurethane Foam.
Quick-recognition process for commercial flat-roof systems: surface color → seam type → surface texture → edge appearance.
Quick-recognition process for residential steep-slope systems: shape and weight → surface texture → tap sound → age and architectural context.
USA Flat Roof's brand for rejuvenation services. Manufacturer-specific product applied to qualifying composition shingle roofs.
Application of mineral granules onto a wet coating to add walkability, aesthetic, or warranty-required surfacing.
Erosion of the protective mineral granules from composition shingle surfaces, indicating aging. Significant granule loss with exposed asphalt is an aging marker.
Channel along the eave of a residential roof that collects rainwater and directs it to downspouts.
Impact damage from hail, characterized by craters, soft spots, or granule displacement on shingles; impact bruising on membranes. Certification requires qualified inspector.
Flashing where a sloped roof meets a vertical wall above (e.g., where a lower roof meets the higher house wall).
Recurring scenario where the rep can take an easier sale (writing a proposal that won't perform, capitulating to a customer's preferred but inappropriate scope) or hold the line on the honest answer.
Adding insulation thickness during a roof recover or replacement, typically to meet code, energy targets, or to add R-value. One of three USA Flat Roof SPF use cases.
Inflow point of an attic ventilation system, typically at the soffit.
Stains, drips, or moisture damage visible from inside the building. Documented during inspection.
Roof drain located within the roof field, with piping carrying water through the building's interior.
Heated asphalt used in hot-mopped BUR or modified bitumen installation. Not used by USA Flat Roof on new installs.
Small piece of flashing at the bottom of a sidewall step flashing line that diverts water away from the wall and into the gutter. Frequently missing on older homes.
Number of distinct roofing systems on a building. Central inspection question on BUR substrates for recover decisions.
Initial conversation with the customer to establish history, concerns, and goals before inspection.
The actual location where water enters the roof assembly. Rarely directly above the visible interior damage.
Warranty term commonly used in marketing for shingles. Typically structured as a non-prorated period followed by prorated coverage that decreases over time.
Subscription or recurring service offering for ongoing roof care. Captures customers who don't need major work today and supports long-term relationship.
Section at the end of each chapter listing items the manager should verify before publication.
Stronger manufacturer warranty available when full system is installed by certified contractor following specifications. NDL = No Dollar Limit.
One-thousandth of an inch. Standard unit for membrane and coating thickness.
Residential home with both pitched and flat-roof sections. Requires both Chapter 17 and Chapter 18 recognition skills.
Asphalt-based multi-ply roofing system with polymer modifiers. Available in self-adhered, torch-down, or hot-mopped installation methods; USA Flat Roof installs only self-adhered.
Lowest severity level on the four-point scale; condition warrants documentation and re-inspection but no immediate action.
Single continuous seamless layer. Characteristic of coatings and SPF.
Mortar used at ridge and hip lines on tile roofs. Subject to deterioration and a common maintenance item.
See manufacturer system warranty.
Characteristic surface texture of cured SPF foam. Most reliable visual identifier.
Secondary drainage at higher elevation than primary, intended to handle water if primary clogs. Required by code on most commercial flat roofs.
Installation of a new roof system over an existing one. Code typically permits one overlay before tear-off becomes required.
Low wall extending above the roof line on a commercial building. Often involves complex flashing details.
Anything that passes through the roof: pipes, vents, drains, conduit, skylights, etc.
Approach where a roof scope is divided into stages, addressing the most urgent items first and planning the broader scope later. Honest alternative when budget doesn't support full scope.
Sealed enclosure used at irregular penetrations on BUR and similar systems. Sealant-dependent; high-failure detail.
Aging mechanism in PVC where plasticizers leach out, causing stiffening and brittleness. Visible on aged PVC (15+ years).
Individual layer of a multi-ply roofing system.
Water remaining on a roof beyond the time intended by design. Acceptable up to 48 hours; problematic beyond.
Dirt accumulation pattern marking where water has been sitting on the roof. Most reliable indicator of past ponding on a dry roof.
Preparatory coat applied before main coating, designed to improve adhesion and substrate compatibility.
The shape and configuration of a roof covering: three-tab, architectural, S-tile, barrel, etc.
Warranty coverage that decreases over time. Common on shingle 'lifetime' warranties.
Single-ply thermoplastic roof membrane; premium positioning; chemical-resistant.
Measure of thermal resistance of insulation. Higher R-value = better insulation.
See overlay.
An object included in a photo to indicate size: business card, tape measure, ruler, chalk mark.
Chemical treatment that absorbs into asphalt shingles to replace lost oils and extend service life. USA Flat Roof's brand: Fresh Roof.
Targeted fix of a specific failure on a roof that otherwise has remaining service life.
Conversation pattern where the rep addresses the immediate issue and surfaces the broader situation honestly.
Full removal of the existing roof down to the deck and installation of a new system.
Adding a layer to extend the useful service life of a roof. Usually a coating; can include rejuvenation.
Course of shingles or specialized component at the peak of a sloped roof.
Continuous exhaust ventilation at the ridge of a sloped roof.
Walk-test on a flat roof to identify soft or spongy areas suggesting wet insulation or substrate failure.
Recurring callout in the book identifying tasks or judgments that exceed the rep's role and require escalation.
Opening through a parapet wall for drainage.
Joint between two pieces of membrane material. Welded on TPO and PVC; asphalt-bonded on modified bitumen; glued or taped on EPDM.
Installation method where the membrane has a peel-and-stick backing. The only modified bitumen installation method USA Flat Roof uses on new installs.
The four-point inspection rating used throughout this book: Monitor, Maintenance Needed, Repair Recommended, Urgent / Escalate.
Pattern where two exhaust types (e.g., gable vents and ridge vents on the same roof) pull from each other instead of pulling air through the full attic.
Flashing where a sloped roof meets a vertical wall to the side. Includes step flashing and counter-flashing.
High-build liquid-applied roof coating using silicone chemistry. Excellent ponding tolerance, UV-resistant. USA Flat Roof's primary restoration product.
Roof system using a single layer of membrane (TPO, PVC, EPDM).
The intentional minor slope (typically 1/4 inch per foot or less) built into flat-roof design to direct water toward drainage.
The horizontal underside of a roof eave, typically containing intake vents on residential homes.
Sprayed foam roofing system, applied with a topcoat. Three USA Flat Roof use cases: ponding correction, BUR recover, insulation upgrade.
Typical sequence of original system + recovers/coatings over time on a commercial roof.
Stepped metal flashing at the sidewall of a sloped roof, with each piece overlapping the one below.
Metal roof panels with stone-coated surface designed to mimic shake or tile. Specialty trade; outside USA Flat Roof's standard scope.
The existing roof beneath a coating, recover, or new system.
Manufactured tile- or slate-look roofing made of polymer, rubber, or composite materials. Lighter than real tile or slate. Limited USA Flat Roof scope.
Visible cutout segments on three-tab composition shingles creating the three-tab pattern.
Light tapping on roofing materials to distinguish concrete (dull thud), clay (ring), synthetic (plastic pop), or metal (metallic).
Insulation manufactured or installed with thickness variation to create slope. Alternative to SPF taper for ponding correction.
Full removal of existing roof down to the deck.
Metal bar that secures the top edge of a flat-roof membrane against a wall or parapet.
Composition shingle with cutouts creating three tab segments per shingle. Older style; less common on new installs.
California's energy efficiency code, including cool-roof requirements that influence membrane and coating color specifications.
UV-protective layer applied over SPF foam. Typically silicone (sometimes acrylic). Required for SPF performance and warranty.
Modified bitumen installation method using a propane torch to melt and bond plies. Not used by USA Flat Roof on new installs.
Single-ply thermoplastic roof membrane; USA Flat Roof's flagship commercial system; 60-mil mechanically attached standard.
Junction where two different roof planes, walls, or systems meet.
Material installed over the deck before the surface roofing material. On tile roofs, the underlayment is the actual waterproofing layer, not the tiles.
Highest severity level on the four-point scale; condition requires immediate action and rep escalation.
Where two sloped roof planes meet creating a low channel for water flow. High-stress area; common leak location.
Recurring callout in the book reminding the rep to confirm code requirements, jurisdiction-specific items, or product details with local authorities or current documentation.
Protective surface installed on flat roofs at high-traffic areas to prevent membrane wear from foot traffic.
Walking on a flat roof while feeling for soft or spongy areas indicating wet insulation or substrate failure.
The three typical warranty layers on a roof: manufacturer material warranty, manufacturer system warranty, contractor workmanship warranty.
Insulation that has absorbed moisture from a leak or vapor migration. Disqualifying condition for restoration.
Leak that occurs only during heavy wind events, often at sidewall transitions, parapets, or sealant joints subject to wind-driven rain.
Contractor-issued warranty covering installation quality. Issued by USA Flat Roof, not the manufacturer.
Areas where development meets wildland fuel. California fire codes restrict certain roofing materials in WUI zones, particularly wood shake.
